🔬 Science

Scientific Learning

Learn how scientists explore and understand the world around us!

📚 Unit 1 ⏱️ 7 min read

Unit 1: Scientific Learning

Science is the study of the natural world through observation and experiment. Scientific learning is the process of discovering new knowledge about nature.

What is Science?

Science comes from the Latin word scientia, meaning “knowledge”.

Science is: A way of asking questions and finding answers about the world around us.

Branches of Science

BranchWhat it studiesExample
PhysicsMatter and energyLight, sound, heat, motion
ChemistrySubstances and their changesMixing, burning, dissolving
BiologyLiving thingsPlants, animals, humans
Earth ScienceEarth and spaceRocks, weather, stars

The Scientific Method

Scientists use a special step-by-step process called the scientific method.

Steps of the Scientific Method

StepWhat to doExample
1. Ask a QuestionWhat do you want to know?Do plants grow faster with sunlight?
2. Make a HypothesisMake an educated guess (prediction)“Plants need sunlight to grow.”
3. Do an ExperimentTest your hypothesisGrow one plant in sun, one in dark
4. Observe and RecordWrite down what happensMeasure plant height every day
5. Draw a ConclusionWhat did you learn?Plants in sunlight grew taller

💡 Hypothesis is a smart guess that can be tested.

Scientific Skills

Good scientists develop these important skills:

SkillWhat it meansExample
ObservationUsing senses to gather informationSeeing that ice melts in the sun
MeasurementUsing tools to get exact dataMeasuring temperature with a thermometer
ClassificationGrouping similar things togetherGrouping animals into mammals, birds, reptiles
PredictionSaying what might happen next”If I add heat, the water will boil.”
ExperimentationTesting ideas carefullyMixing vinegar and baking soda
CommunicationSharing findings with othersWriting a report or giving a talk

Science Laboratory and Safety

A laboratory (lab) is a special room where scientists do experiments.

Lab Safety Rules

RuleWhy it is important
Listen to the teacher firstYou will know what to do safely
Wear safety gogglesProtect your eyes from splashes
Do not run in the labYou might spill or break something
Do not taste or smell chemicalsThey could be poisonous
Wash your hands after experimentsRemove any chemicals from your skin
Keep your workspace cleanPrevents accidents
Tell the teacher about spillsThey can clean it safely

Common Lab Equipment

EquipmentUse
BeakerHolding and measuring liquids
Test tubeMixing small amounts of chemicals
MicroscopeSeeing very tiny things
ThermometerMeasuring temperature
Magnifying glassMaking small things look bigger
Measuring cylinderMeasuring liquid volume accurately

⚠️ Warning: Never touch, taste, or smell chemicals unless your teacher tells you to!

Observation and Measurement

Using Your Senses for Observation

SenseWhat you can observe
SightColor, shape, size, movement
HearingSound, loudness, pitch
TouchTexture, temperature, hardness
SmellOdor (only when told to!)
TasteFlavor (only safe things!)

Units of Measurement

Scientists use standard units called SI units (International System).

What we measureUnitSymbolTool
Lengthmetermruler, measuring tape
Massgram, kilogramg, kgweighing scale
Volumeliter, milliliterL, mLmeasuring cylinder
Temperaturedegree Celsius°Cthermometer
Timesecond, minute, hours, min, hrclock, stopwatch

Science in Daily Life

Science is not just in laboratories – it is everywhere!

Daily ActivityScience Concept
Cooking foodHeat transfers, chemical changes
Riding a bicycleForce, motion, balance
Using a mobile phoneElectricity, sound waves
Growing plantsPhotosynthesis, germination
Boiling waterStates of matter (liquid to gas)
Weather forecastAir pressure, temperature, humidity

💡 Did you know? A refrigerator uses science to keep food cold. A fan uses electricity to create air movement. Science is all around you!

Important Scientists to Know

ScientistFamous for
Isaac NewtonLaws of motion, gravity
Albert EinsteinTheory of relativity
Marie CurieRadioactivity
Charles DarwinEvolution
Galileo GalileiTelescope, astronomy
Louis PasteurGerm theory, pasteurization

Try It Yourself!

Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks

  1. Science comes from the Latin word ____________.
  2. The first step of the scientific method is to ask a ____________.
  3. A ____________ is an educated guess.
  4. The SI unit of length is the ____________.
  5. A ____________ is used to see very tiny things.

Exercise 2: True or False

  1. You should run in the science laboratory. (T/F)
  2. Observation means using your senses. (T/F)
  3. Biology is the study of rocks. (T/F)
  4. A thermometer measures temperature. (T/F)
  5. You should taste chemicals to identify them. (T/F)

Exercise 3: Match the Tool to Its Use

ToolUse
Microscopea) Measure temperature
Thermometerb) See tiny objects
Measuring cylinderc) Measure liquid volume
Beakerd) Hold and pour liquids
Magnifying glasse) Make small things look bigger

Exercise 4: Think Like a Scientist

Imagine you want to know: Does salt dissolve faster in hot water or cold water?

Write the steps of the scientific method for this question:

  1. Question: ________________________________
  2. Hypothesis: ________________________________
  3. Experiment: ________________________________
  4. Observation: ________________________________
  5. Conclusion: ________________________________

Answer Key

Exercise 1: 1. scientia, 2. question, 3. hypothesis, 4. meter, 5. microscope

Exercise 2: 1. F, 2. T, 3. F, 4. T, 5. F

Exercise 3: Microscope→b, Thermometer→a, Measuring cylinder→c, Beaker→d, Magnifying glass→e

Great work! Now you know how scientists learn new things. Keep asking questions and exploring the world around you!
